name: typescript-patterns
description: Common TypeScript patterns, gotchas, and best practices

observations.title: Use 'satisfies' for type-safe config objects
observations.narrative: The 'satisfies' operator validates a value matches a type without widening it. Prefer `const config = { ... } satisfies Config` over `const config: Config = { ... }` to preserve literal types.

observations.title: Discriminated unions over optional properties
observations.narrative: Use a shared literal 'kind' or 'type' field to discriminate union variants. This enables exhaustive switch/case checking and eliminates impossible states that optional properties allow.

observations.title: Use 'as const' for literal type inference
observations.narrative: Apply 'as const' to object/array literals when you need TypeScript to infer exact string/number literal types instead of widened string/number types.

observations.title: Template literal types for string patterns
observations.narrative: TypeScript supports template literal types like `type Route = \`/api/${string}\`` to constrain string shapes at the type level. Useful for API paths, CSS values, and event names.

observations.title: Avoid 'any' leaking through generic defaults
observations.narrative: Generic functions with `<T = any>` silently disable type checking for callers who don't provide the type parameter. Use `<T = unknown>` or `<T extends Base>` instead.

observations.title: Branded types for domain identifiers
observations.narrative: Prevent mixing up IDs like userId and orderId by creating branded types: `type UserId = string & { __brand: 'UserId' }`. Create helper functions to construct them.

observations.title: Use 'unknown' over 'any' for safe catch blocks
observations.narrative: In catch blocks, use `catch (err: unknown)` and narrow with `err instanceof Error` before accessing .message. This prevents runtime crashes from non-Error throws.

observations.title: Record<string, T> vs Map<string, T>
observations.narrative: Use Record for static key sets known at compile time. Use Map when keys are dynamic, when you need size/iteration order guarantees, or when non-string keys are needed.

observations.title: Infer return types from implementation
observations.narrative: For internal functions, let TypeScript infer the return type from the implementation. Only add explicit return types at module boundaries (exports) or when the inference is too wide.

observations.title: Zod schemas as single source of truth
observations.narrative: Define Zod schemas first, then derive TypeScript types with z.infer<typeof schema>. This gives you both runtime validation and compile-time types from a single definition.
